title: Level 11: Serial Comm (UART Receive Interrupts)
aim: To construct an interrupt-driven UART receiver that automatically catches characters arriving via Serial without polling.
objective: Understand the RXCIE0 interrupt, safely buffer incoming characters inside the ISR(USART_RX_vect), and securely pass them to the main loop.
theory:
The Problem with Polling Rx

Unlike transmitting (Tx), where you decide when to send, receiving (Rx) is totally unpredictable. A user could type a character at any microsecond. If you use a blocking `while(!(UCSR0A & (1<

The RX Complete Interrupt

By enabling the Receiver Interrupt Enable (RXCIE0) bit in UCSR0B, the UART hardware will automatically trigger an interrupt the instant a new byte completely finishes shifting into the ATmega328P. You write an ISR(USART_RX_vect) that reads the UDR0 register and saves the character to a `volatile` buffer (like an array or ring buffer).

Interrupt Handlers

When the ISR fires, global interrupts are momentarily disabled. You must read UDR0 inside the ISR immediately, or the next incoming character might overwrite it before your main loop processes it.

pretest.question: Which interrupt vector is responsible for firing when a new character arrives on the UART Rx pin?
pretest.explanation: USART_RX_vect triggers when a full 8-bit frame has successfully been assembled in the UDR0 hardware buffer.

pretest.question: What is the primary purpose of the DDRx register in the ATmega328P?
options: To configure pins as input or output
pretest.explanation: DDR configures the direction of each GPIO pin.

pretest.question: Which header file provides basic I/O register definitions for AVR?
pretest.explanation: <avr/io.h> provides register definitions for all AVR peripherals.

pretest.question: What does the volatile keyword tell the C compiler?
options: May change unexpectedly, so do not optimize reads
pretest.explanation: volatile prevents the compiler from caching the variable.

pretest.question: How many GPIO pins does the ATmega328P have in total?
pretest.explanation: 23 GPIO pins across Port B, C, and D.

pretest.question: What is the maximum clock frequency of the ATmega328P?
pretest.explanation: Supports up to 20MHz, though Arduino UNO uses 16MHz.

pretest.question: What is the purpose of sei()?
pretest.explanation: sei() sets the global interrupt enable flag in SREG.

pretest.question: Which AVR memory type retains data after power-off?
What is the width of each ATmega328P port register?
pretest.explanation: The ATmega328P is an 8-bit MCU; all port registers are 8 bits wide.

pretest.question: What is bit manipulation?
options: Operating on individual bits using bitwise operators
pretest.explanation: Bit manipulation uses AND, OR, XOR, and SHIFT.

procedure: Configure UBRR0 for your serial baud rate (e.g., 9600).
Enable BOTH the Receiver (RXEN0) and the Transceiver (TXEN0) in UCSR0B.
Enable the Receiver Interrupt (RXCIE0) in UCSR0B.
Declare a `volatile unsigned char last_rx_char;` and a `volatile uint8_t char_ready;` flag globally.
Call sei(); to allow global interrupts to fire.
Write ISR(USART_RX_vect). Inside, read the incoming byte from UDR0 into `last_rx_char`, and set `char_ready = 1;`.
In your main loop, continuously check if `char_ready` is 1.
When `char_ready` is 1, clear it to 0, and transmit the received character back out so you can see it on the monitor (this is called Echoing).

posttest.question: What happens if you never read UDR0 inside an enabled Receive Interrupt ISR?
options: The ISR re-triggers instantly and endlessly, freezing the system
It sends the character back to the computer automatically
posttest.explanation: The hardware keeps the RXC0 flag high until UDR0 is read. If the ISR exits without reading it, the interrupt mechanism instantly fires the ISR again.

posttest.question: Why is it good practice to use #define for pin numbers?
posttest.explanation: #define creates named constants for self-documenting code.

posttest.question: What tool converts C source to .hex for AVR?
posttest.explanation: avr-gcc compiles C to AVR machine code.

posttest.question: What is the difference between PORTB and PINB?
options: PORTB writes output; PINB reads actual pin voltages
posttest.explanation: PORTB drives outputs; PINB reads real-time pin states.

posttest.question: What happens to SRAM when power is removed?
What is the stack used for in AVR?
posttest.explanation: Stack stores return addresses and local variables.

posttest.question: Which is NOT a valid ATmega328P port?
posttest.explanation: ATmega328P has only Port B, C, and D. No Port A.

posttest.question: What is a watchdog timer used for?
posttest.explanation: WDT auto-resets MCU when not periodically fed.

posttest.question: What does the SREG register contain?
posttest.explanation: SREG holds CPU flags and the global interrupt enable bit.

posttest.question: In an ISR, interrupts are automatically:
posttest.explanation: AVR clears I-flag in SREG upon entering an ISR.

feedback: Outstanding asynchronous code. This non-blocking reception technique is the core behind Bluetooth, GPS, and Wi-Fi modules communicating with arduinos.
